questions.question_text: What must appear on every real estate advertisement in Delaware?
distractors: The salesperson's license number
The MLS listing identification
The brokerage phone number only
questions.explanation: Commission Rule 8.6.5.3 requires every ad for listed property to include the complete brokerage organization name registered with the Commission and the brokerage phone number registered for that office, displayed more prominently (greater size and visibility) than the licensee's name. The same requirement applies in every medium, including print, online, digital, and social media, so consumers can identify the responsible brokerage.

questions.question_text: An agent posts a listing on social media using only the team name and their personal contact info. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage organization name is missing
distractors: Yes, team names satisfy the requirement
Yes, if the agent's license number is included
Only non-compliant for print advertising
questions.explanation: All advertising, including social media, must include the registered brokerage organization name and phone number (Rule 8.6.5.3). Team names are permitted, but team advertising must prominently display the supervising broker's brokerage organization name adjacent to the team name, in greater size and visibility than the team name (Rule 8.6.5.4). An ad showing only a team name and personal contact info conceals the responsible brokerage and is prohibited.

questions.question_text: What is a 'blind ad' in Delaware real estate?
questions.correct_answer: An ad without the broker's identity
distractors: An ad for off-market properties only
An ad placed by an unlicensed person
An ad with no property address listed
questions.explanation: A blind ad is any advertisement that does not identify the responsible brokerage. Delaware prohibits such ads because they prevent consumers from knowing who is responsible for the advertisement and the transaction. Delaware's rules accomplish this by requiring every ad for listed property, and every personal-promotion ad, to display the complete brokerage organization name registered with the Commission along with the registered brokerage phone number (Rules 8.6.5.3, 8.6.5.5).

questions.question_text: A real estate team creates a website with a unique team brand name. Which statement about their advertising is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Team name must include broker identification
distractors: Team names replace broker name requirements
Team websites are exempt from ad rules
Only the team leader's name is needed
questions.explanation: Team names are allowed in Delaware advertising, but they must not be misleading and must include brokerage identification. The team name itself must include 'team,' 'group,' or 'associates' and may not contain terms like 'Realty' or 'Real Estate' that suggest a separate brokerage (Rule 8.5.2); the brokerage organization name must appear adjacent to the team name in greater size and visibility (Rule 8.6.5.4).

questions.question_text: A licensee advertises a property on a digital billboard. Do the same advertising rules apply as for print ads?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, identical rules for all ad formats
distractors: No, digital ads have separate standards
Only if the billboard is within Delaware
Digital ads only need a website link
questions.explanation: Delaware applies the same advertising rules across all media formats: print, online, digital, social media, and billboards. The registered brokerage organization name must appear regardless of the advertising medium used. Internet advertising has one variation: the required disclosures may be provided via a link, and the ad must also identify the city and state of the broker's main office and the broker's jurisdiction of licensure (Rule 8.6.5.6).

questions.question_text: A buyer asks a licensee to draft a purchase agreement from scratch. What should the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline and refer to an attorney
distractors: Draft it if the broker approves
Draft it using standard legal language
Draft it and have the buyer review
questions.explanation: Delaware licensees cannot draft legal documents. They may only fill in blanks on pre-approved forms. Drafting a purchase agreement from scratch constitutes the unauthorized practice of law. The licensee should refer the buyer to a Delaware attorney.

questions.question_text: A client asks their agent whether a contract clause protects them from liability. How should the agent respond?
distractors: Explain the clause based on experience
Interpret the clause using training materials
Provide a written legal opinion for review
questions.explanation: Interpreting contract clauses and advising on legal protections constitutes giving legal advice, which is prohibited for licensees. Delaware is an attorney state where lawyers handle closings and legal interpretations. The agent should refer the client to a licensed attorney.

questions.question_text: Which action is permitted for a Delaware real estate licensee when preparing transaction documents?
questions.correct_answer: Filling in blanks on approved forms
distractors: Drafting custom contract addenda
Modifying pre-printed legal clauses
Writing legal descriptions from surveys
questions.explanation: Delaware licensees may only fill in the blanks on approved forms. They cannot draft custom documents, modify legal clauses, or create legal descriptions. Delaware is an attorney state, meaning attorneys typically handle closings and document preparation beyond basic form completion.

questions.question_text: Why is Delaware classified as an 'attorney state' for real estate transactions?
questions.correct_answer: Attorneys handle closings and legal work
distractors: Only attorneys can hold broker licenses
Attorneys must approve all listing contracts
The bar association regulates licensees
questions.explanation: Delaware is an attorney state because attorneys are required to handle real estate closings and legal document preparation. Licensees assist with the transaction but cannot perform legal functions such as drafting documents, interpreting clauses, or providing legal opinions.

questions.question_text: An experienced agent adds a custom warranty clause to a standard contract form. What risk does this create?
distractors: A minor advertising rule infraction
No risk if the broker reviews it
A disclosure timing violation only
questions.explanation: Adding custom legal language to a contract goes beyond filling in blanks on approved forms. This constitutes the unauthorized practice of law in Delaware, regardless of the agent's experience level. Only a licensed attorney may draft or add legal clauses to contracts.

questions.question_text: A licensee offers a valuable prize drawing to homeowners who list with her firm. Under Delaware law, how is this classified?
distractors: A standard marketing promotion
A permitted referral incentive
Legal if prize value is disclosed
questions.explanation: Delaware's inducement rule (Rule 8.8.1) bars licensees from using commission income to compensate unlicensed persons as an inducement to secure business. Rule 8.8.2 does permit rebates or discounts given directly to a purchaser or seller in a transaction, but a prize drawing offered to attract listings is not a transaction rebate — it is a chance-based inducement to win business, and conditioning entry on listing adds the consideration element of an illegal lottery. Such promotions are therefore potentially improper inducements.

questions.question_text: A licensee prepares a comparative market analysis for a seller. What must the licensee avoid?
questions.correct_answer: Presenting the CMA as a formal appraisal
distractors: Using recent comparable sales data
Providing the CMA in writing
Sharing the CMA with the seller
questions.explanation: Delaware law states flatly that a competitive market analysis is not an appraisal, and 24 Del. C. §2931 requires each CMA to carry a 12-point boldface disclosure, immediately after the estimated price, stating it is NOT an appraisal and is not to be used for any legal purpose. If an appraisal is needed, a licensed or certified appraiser must be engaged.

questions.question_text: A licensee who is not a certified appraiser labels their CMA report as an 'Official Property Appraisal.' What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Misrepresenting a CMA as an appraisal
distractors: No violation if the data is accurate
A minor labeling technicality only
A violation only if used for lending
questions.explanation: Labeling a CMA as an appraisal when the licensee is not a licensed appraiser is a serious violation. Only a licensed or certified appraiser may prepare a document held out as an appraisal; §2931's mandatory disclosure states that if an appraisal is desired, 'the services of a licensed or certified appraiser must be obtained.' This misrepresentation can mislead consumers about the document's reliability and legal standing.

questions.question_text: A licensee pays a neighbor $200 cash for each homeowner she refers who lists with the licensee. What violation does this represent?
questions.correct_answer: An illegal referral fee to an unlicensed person
distractors: A permitted finder's fee arrangement
Legal if the neighbor signs a contract
Acceptable if disclosed to the seller
questions.explanation: Paying compensation to unlicensed persons for referrals violates Delaware real estate law. Only licensed individuals may receive compensation for real estate referrals. This type of arrangement falls under unfair inducements and can result in disciplinary action against the licensee.

questions.question_text: When preparing a CMA, which guideline must a Delaware licensee follow?
distractors: Follow USPAP appraisal standards only
Include a formal appraiser certification
Submit the CMA to the state for approval
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2931 a CMA may be prepared only for an existing or potential seller/owner (for listing) or buyer/tenant (for purchase or lease) — never for a mortgagee on a property already under a signed agreement of sale. It must include the statutory 12-point boldface disclosure, placed immediately after the estimated market price, that it is not an appraisal, and its content must comply with Commission regulations. USPAP standards govern appraisals, not CMAs.
